subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Elected Legislature vs. Independent Judiciary in Preserving Limited Government
stimulus: There is continued debate over how to best preserve the democratic ideal of limited government. Develop an argument as to whether an elected legislature or an independent judiciary is more effective in preserving limited government.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Federalist No. 51, Federalist No. 78, Article I of the Constitution of the United States.)
parts.scoring_notes: Must take a clear position. Generic statements about limited government being debated do not earn the point. Saying 'the executive branch best protects limited government' does not earn the point (not responsive to the prompt).

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “An elected legislature is more effective at preserving limited government because they are elected by the majority and are held accountable to the will of the people.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “An elected legislature is better able to preserve limited government because checks and balances allow the legislature to limit the other branches of government.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “An independent judiciary is better able to preserve limited government because checks and balances allow the judiciary to limit the other branches of government.“

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “The independent judiciary is more effective at preserving limited government because they are not accountable to the wishes of the majority.”

wrong_examples: “There are debates about whether an elected legislature or an independent judiciary is more effective in preserving limited government.” Do not respond to the prompt
“The executive branch best protects limited government.”
common_traps: Restating the prompt without making a claim
Describing the issue without taking a position

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 3 points, must have earned Row A, and one piece of evidence must come from Federalist 51, Federalist 78, or Article I.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (0 points): No accurate evidence or evidence not relevant to the topic.

required_elements: No accurate or relevant evidence
alternatives.correct_example: No points earned — evidence is inaccurate or irrelevant to the prompt.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (1 point): Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: Must provide one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.
alternatives.correct_example: Must provide one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.

common_traps: Evidence must be relevant but need not directly support a specific claim to earn 1 point

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (2 points): Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim/thesis OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence.

required_elements: Provide one piece of specific and relevant evidence that supports the claim or thesis. OR
alternatives.correct_example: Provide one piece of specific and relevant evidence that supports the claim or thesis. OR

required_elements: Must provide two pieces of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.
alternatives.correct_example: Must provide two pieces of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.

common_traps: To earn 2 points, the response must have earned Row A (claim/thesis)

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (3 points): Uses two pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim, one from a listed foundational document.

required_elements: Provide two pieces of specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt. The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. Examples that do not earn points: Provide evidence that is not specific
alternatives.correct_example: Provide two pieces of specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt. The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. Examples that do not earn points: Provide evidence that is not specific

required_elements: “Legislatures have representatives.” Provide evidence that is not relevant to the topic of the prompt
alternatives.correct_example: “Legislatures have representatives.” Provide evidence that is not relevant to the topic of the prompt

required_elements: “The Constitutional Convention of 1787 was held in Philadelphia.” Examples of evidence that are relevant to the topic of the prompt:
alternatives.correct_example: “The Constitutional Convention of 1787 was held in Philadelphia.” Examples of evidence that are relevant to the topic of the prompt:

required_elements: “The principle of republicanism is found in the design of our government.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The principle of republicanism is found in the design of our government.”

required_elements: “Article III of the Constitution establishes the judicial branch of government.” Examples of acceptable specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis (one example is one piece of evidence):
alternatives.correct_example: “Article III of the Constitution establishes the judicial branch of government.” Examples of acceptable specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis (one example is one piece of evidence):

required_elements: “Legislative processes are conducted by officials who are directly elected by a majority of citizens.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Legislative processes are conducted by officials who are directly elected by a majority of citizens.”

required_elements: “Article III establishes that federal judicial officials serve for a term of good behavior.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Article III establishes that federal judicial officials serve for a term of good behavior.”

required_elements: “Federalist No. 51 discusses how each branch can check the power of the other branches.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Federalist No. 51 discusses how each branch can check the power of the other branches.”

required_elements: “Federalist No. 78 explains that the judicial branch is not elected and holds lifetime appointments.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Federalist No. 78 explains that the judicial branch is not elected and holds lifetime appointments.”

required_elements: “Article I establishes the legislative branch as well as qualifications for holding office, elections to the office, and the powers of the office.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Article I establishes the legislative branch as well as qualifications for holding office, elections to the office, and the powers of the office.”

common_traps: One piece must be from a foundational document listed in the prompt

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have provided at least one piece of specific and relevant evidence. Must explain the logical connection between evidence and the thesis.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Article I establishes the legislative branch as well as qualifications for holding office, elections to the office, and the powers of the office. Article I preserves limited government because it allows voters to respond to decisions made by the legislature through elections.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Federalist No. 51 discusses how each branch can check the power of the other branches. A legislative override of a presidential veto is one way to check the power of the president and preserve limited government.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Federalist No. 51 discusses how each branch can check the power of the other branches. The Supreme Court preserves limited government through the use of judicial review, which allows them to decide if laws passed by Congress or executive orders issued by the president are constitutional.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Federalist No. 78 explains that the judicial branch is not elected and holds lifetime appointments. The judicial branch protects limited government because it is insulated from political pressures and can protect against the abuse of power by other branches.”

common_traps: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B
Simply restating evidence without reasoning does not earn this point

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut or refute it — not merely acknowledge it.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “Some people might say that an independent judiciary would best preserve limited government because they are not influenced by public opinion. However, an elected legislature better protects limited government because elections make them responsive to the will of the people.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “It can be argued that an elected legislature is more likely to preserve limited government because they are elected by their constituents. However, since an independent judiciary is not elected, they are not subject to the will of the people and therefore more likely to preserve limited government.“

wrong_examples: “An elected legislature is also a strong way to protect limited government.” Describe an alternate perspective but do not rebut or refute that perspective
“Some may argue the executive branch is the best protector of limited government, which I would agree.”
common_traps: Simply identifying an alternate perspective without rebutting/refuting it does not earn this point
